coredata.dc:title: Mechanism of ultrasonic impregnation on porosity of activated carbons in non-cavitation and cavitation regimes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zongbo
Liu, Xiaoyang
Li, Dawei
Lei, Yuqi
Gao, Tiantian
Wu, Baogui
Zhao, Jiawei
Wang, Yankui
Zhou, Guangyan
Yao, Huimin
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasonic impregnation has proven to be an effective method to improve surface area and pore volume during preparation of activated carbons. However, the mechanism by which the promotion effect of ultrasonic impregnation is still ambiguous. Fundamental wave pressure (FWP) and broadband integrated pressure (BIP) were used to estimate the non-cavitation (vibration) energy and cavitation energy, respectively. The effects of FWP and BIP on the pore volume, surface area, surface functional groups, and microcosmic morphology were investigated in non-cavitation and cavitation regimes. Ultrasonic vibration promoted the surface enlargement and pore development of activated carbons, and it mainly affected the development of mesopore volume (V mes) in both the pore volume and the mesopore-size-distribution range. The V mes was enhanced by 60%–100% in the non-cavitation regime. Ultrasonic cavitation also facilitated porosity development of activated carbons, and it mainly affected the development of specific surface area (S BET) and micropore volume (V mic). The excessive cavitation led to a decrease of the porosity of activated carbons, so the BIP should be optimized during impregnation. The highest S BET, V mic, and V mes for activated carbons were obtained by in the presence of both FWP and BIP, which were enhanced by 29.05%, 30.23%, and 113.33%, respectively, compared with the corresponding value for the activated carbon prepared without using ultrasonic impregnation. This work provided new insight into the role of the acoustic energy present during impregnation in tuning properties of activated carbons.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Activated carbon
H3PO4
Ultrasound
Acoustic energy separation
Ultrasonic vibration
Ultrasonic cavitation

Analysis of structural properties of activated carbon
Characteristics of different acoustic field based on energy separation
Effects of ultrasound during impregnation on pore structure of activated carbons
Effects of non-cavitation activity on surface area and pore volume
Effects of cavitation energy on surface area and pore volume
Effects of ultrasound on surface functional groups of activated carbons
Effects of ultrasound on microcosmic morphology of activated carbons

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic reactors: (a) the horn-based reactor and (b) the bath-based reactor.
Experimental setting for measuring apparatus of sound pressure: (a) the horn- based ultrasonic reactor and (b) the bath-based ultrasonic reactor.
Example of sound pressure spectrum at 20 kHz and 180 W.
Acoustic energy of horn-based ultrasonic reactor at different input power.
Acoustic energy of bath-based ultrasonic reactor at different input power.
Example of sound pressure spectrum at 20 kHz and 6 W.
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (a) and DFT pore size distribution (b) of B-AC series activated carbons and AC-0.
Relevance of FWP and S BET, V mic, and V mes of B-AC series activated carbons.
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (a) and DFT pore size distribution (b) of H-AC series activated carbons.
Relevance of BIP and S BET, V mic, and V mes of H-AC series activated carbons.
FTIR spectra of obtained activated carbons.
SEM images of obtained activated carbons.
Pore parameters of activated carbons prepared by impregnation in presence of different acoustic energy.
Particle size distribution of sawdust before and after ultrasonic irradiation.
Pore parameters of activated carbons prepared in absence or presence of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Mechanism of ultrasonic impregnation on porosity of activated carbons in non-cavitation and cavitation regimes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Development Corporation, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao 266033, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic impregnation has proven to be an effective method to improve surface area and pore volume during preparation of activated carbons. However, the mechanism by which the promotion effect of ultrasonic impregnation is still ambiguous. Fundamental wave pressure (FWP) and broadband integrated pressure (BIP) were used to estimate the non-cavitation (vibration) energy and cavitation energy, respectively. The effects of FWP and BIP on the pore volume, surface area, surface functional groups, and microcosmic morphology were investigated in non-cavitation and cavitation regimes. Ultrasonic vibration promoted the surface enlargement and pore development of activated carbons, and it mainly affected the development of mesopore volume (V mes) in both the pore volume and the mesopore-size-distribution range. The V mes was enhanced by 60%–100% in the non-cavitation regime. Ultrasonic cavitation also facilitated porosity development of activated carbons, and it mainly affected the development of specific surface area (S BET) and micropore volume (V mic). The excessive cavitation led to a decrease of the porosity of activated carbons, so the BIP should be optimized during impregnation. The highest S BET, V mic, and V mes for activated carbons were obtained by in the presence of both FWP and BIP, which were enhanced by 29.05%, 30.23%, and 113.33%, respectively, compared with the corresponding value for the activated carbon prepared without using ultrasonic impregnation. This work provided new insight into the role of the acoustic energy present during impregnation in tuning properties of activated carbons.

ce:section.ce:para: Activated carbon (AC) is a versatile porous material with stable physicochemical properties. It can be used as adsorbents, capacitor electrode, catalysts, and catalyst supports [1–4], because of its developed porosity, large surface area, and tunable surface chemistry. H3PO4 is a widely used chemical activator for preparation of AC in industry, because it possesses merits of less toxic and more environmentally-friendly than ZnCl2, low activation temperature, and high security. However, the traditional method for preparation of H3PO4-activated carbons suffers from long impregnation time, low surface area, and small pore volume. Thus, it is necessary to find new efficient routes to prepare activated carbon with developed pore structure.

Ultrasonic impregnation before chemical activation has proven to be an effective method for preparation of activated carbons. For example, Enes Şayan [5] and Branka V. Kaludjerovic [6] applied ultrasonic irradiation to the impregnation step for preparation of activated carbon and found that the introduction of ultrasound to the impregnation process was quite beneficial to increase surface area and adsorption capacity of activated carbons. Unfortunately, the mechanism for gaining the benefit from ultrasonic impregnation is less understood. The benefit of sonochemical synthesis of materials was usually attributed to the occurrence of ultrasonic cavitation [7–10], which means the formation, growth, pulsation, and collapse of micro-bubbles in liquid. Nevertheless, this explanation is doubtful when it uses to interpret the performance improvement of activated carbons, as we observed that violent cavitation activity led an obvious decrease in surface area of activated carbons in our experiments.

This research focuses on studying the effects of ultrasonic cavitation and non-cavitation activities during impregnation on properties of H3PO4-activated carbon with the aim to figure out the mechanism of ultrasonic impregnation. The cavitation energy and non-cavitation energy were quantified by broadband integrated pressure (BIP) and fundamental wave pressure (FWP), respectively; the BIP and FWP were obtained from sound pressure measurement and subsequent frequency spectra analysis [11]. The properties of activated carbons were characterized in terms of pore structure, surface functional groups, and microcosmic morphology. The research was believed to provide new insight into the role of the acoustic energy present during impregnation in tuning properties of activated carbons.

The sawdust obtained from China Zhucheng Zhongli Corporation, was sieved to sizes of 0.3–0.9 mm and dried at 110 °C for 8 h to get rid of the moisture and other volatile impurities. The carbon content and hydrogen content of the dried sawdust were 47.1 wt% and 6.4 wt%, respectively.

ACs were produced from sawdust through the ultrasonic-assisted impregnation followed by thermal activation. Firstly, 3.5 g of dehydrated sawdust was impregnated in 28 mL of 15 wt% H3PO4 solution for 45 min under different ultrasonic powers with ultrasonic time of 5 min. Two types of ultrasonic reactors (horn-based and bath-based) were employed in the impregnation, as shown in Fig. 1 . The mixture of sawdust and H3PO4 solution was then dried in an oven at 110 °C for 2.5 h to eliminate water. Subsequently, the dried mixture was activated in a tube furnace (SLG1100-60, China) at 425 °C for 120 min under flowing N2 of 400 mL/min. The obtained materials were rinsed with distilled water until the pH became neutral, and finally dried at 110 °C for 10 h to constant weight.

Two different series of activated carbons were prepared, which are referred to as H-AC-x and B-AC-x, where H denoted the horn-based ultrasonic reactor (FS-600N, China), B denoted the bath-based ultrasonic reactor, and x referred to the power of ultrasonic reactor in Watt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of acoustic energy in the reactor
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental apparatus for the measurement of sound pressure is shown in Fig. 2 . A horn-based ultrasonic reactor (20 kHz) was used with the ultrasonic horn immersed into the mixture of sawdust and H3PO4 solution in a beaker, as demonstrated in Fig. 2a. A Langevin transducer (20 kHz) with diameter of 59 mm was fixed at the bottom of the stainless steel vessel (bath-based ultrasonic reactor); ultrasound was irradiated onto the mixture of sawdust and H3PO4 solution in the vessel, as shown in Fig. 2b.

Acoustic pressure measurements were conducted using a hydrophone (TC4034; RESON A/S, Slangerup, Denmark). The hydrophone was fixed using a self-made bracket to guarantee the position accuracy. The acoustic signals collected from hydrophone were recorded by a digital oscilloscope (DS2102, RIGOL, China) and then converted to sound pressure using signal processing module. The measurement was carried out in six different locations and each measurement was repeated five times to compensate the error caused by the randomness of cavitation. The average of the six locations was calculated to characterize the acoustic energy in the reactor.

Ultrasonic energy was divided into non-cavitation energy and cavitation energy [12]. The non-cavitation energy is evaluated by the fundamental wave pressure (FWP), which is mainly derived from the ultrasonic vibration [13]. Maximal value near input fundamental frequency was regarded as the actual fundamental frequency (f1), and the frequency band of fundamental wave is defined as f 1 ± 2.5 kHz. The cavitation energy is estimated from the broadband integrated pressure (BIP) [14–17], which is mainly derived from the radiation force and acoustic micro-streaming produced by the fluctuation of cavitation bubbles, and the shock wave emitted from the collapse of cavitation bubbles.

Fig. 3 shows an example of a sound pressure spectrum obtained in our study (horn-based ultrasonic reactor). FWP and BIP were calculated using the following equation:
(1)
F W P =
∫
f
s 1
f
e 1
P S
f 1
-
P N
f
d f
(2)
B I P =
∫
f s
f e
P S
f
-
P N
f
d f
where P S(f 1) represents the fundamental wave pressure, P S(f) represents the fitting broadband sound pressures eliminating fundamental component (the upper red curve), and P N(f) represents the fitting sound pressures of background noise (the lower red curve). The start (f s1) and end (f e1) frequencies of fundamental wave region were at f 1 − 2.5 kHz and f 1 + 2.5 kHz, respectively. The start (f s) and end (f e) frequencies of broadband integration region were at 20 kHz and 500 KHz, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of structural properties of activated carbon
ce:section.ce:para: The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of activated carbon was collected on a static volumetric analyzer (Micromeritics, ASAP 2460, USA) at 77 k. The specific surface area (S BET) was calculated in the P/P0 range of 0.05–0.2 by the Brunauer-Emmet–Teller (BET) equation. The micropore volume (V mic) was measured by Dubinin-Astakhov (DA) method. The total pore volume (V tot) was estimated from the amount of N2 adsorbed at relative pressure (P/P0) of 0.99. The pore size distribution was determined by applying nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT) to adsorption isotherm. FTIR spectra of activated carbons were analyzed using a FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Tensor 27, Germany) in wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 with the resolution of 4 cm−1. Surface morphology of activated carbons were analyzed by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of different acoustic field based on energy separation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the acoustic energy in the horn-based ultrasonic reactor measured under different input power. As the input power increased, the non-cavitation energy increased monotonously. In contrast, the cavitation energy first rose and then decreased, with the maximum BIP of 120.75 kPa·kHz reached at input power of 360 W, which showed that the cavitation energy did not necessarily rise with the enhancement of input power. The decrease of cavitation energy at input power above 360 W might be explained by the rise in the quantity of large bubbles in the sawdust-H3PO4 mixture, which enhanced scattering and absorbance of ultrasound. Previous researchers also reported that the cavitation energy decreased when the input power of the ultrasonic reactor exceeded a certain value [18], which agreed with our aforementioned variation trend of cavitation energy.

Overall, cavitation activity is obviously observed in the horn-based ultrasonic system, even if at the lowest input power. Thus, the acoustic field obtained in this case is a typical cavitation field, in which both cavitation energy and vibration energy are relatively high.

After obtaining the above-mentioned cavitation field, a non-cavitation acoustic filed was needed before we can explore the effects of cavitation activity and vibration activity on properties of activated carbon. Thus, we analyzed the acoustic field in a bath-based ultrasonic reactor at input powers of 6–50 W, with the results presented in Fig. 5 . As the ultrasonic power rose from 6 W to 50 W, the non-cavitation energy increased markedly from 0.65 to 13.21 kPa·kHz, but the cavitation energy was quite low and its variation energy was insignificant, which indicated that the non-cavitation energy is the dominant acoustic energy in this bath-based ultrasonic reactor. More specifically, the sound pressure spectrum at input power of 6 W (Fig. 6 ) showed that the two red curves (represent P S(f) and P N(f)) almost overlap, indicating that no cavitation occurs in this case; furthermore, the maximum BIP (1.16 kPa·kHz) at input power of 50 W remains less than 10% of its corresponding FWP. In a word, the obtained acoustic field in this bath-based ultrasonic reactor is a typical non-cavitation field. The formation of such a non-cavitation field was attributable to the low input power and high viscosity of H3PO4-sawdust mixture, which increased the oscillation period of the bubbles and reduced the collapse intensity [19–21].

In summary, the horn-based ultrasonic system offered a typical cavitation field characterized by the presence of marked cavitation energy, whereas the bath-based ultrasonic system provided a typical vibration field featuring negligible cavitation energy. The availability of the two different fields allowed subsequent study on the effects of cavitation and vibration on the properties of activated carbons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ultrasound during impregnation on pore structure of activated carbons
ce:section.ce:para: Impregnation significantly influences the porosity of activated carbons [22–24]. When the sawdust contacted phosphoric acid, the reactions between them began [25]. H3PO4 attacked the cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin in sawdust, causing hydrolysis of these biopolymers. The biopolymer fragments were connected with H3PO4 via cyclization and condensation to form phosphate and polyphosphate bridges [26–29]. Consequently, new polymeric structure (sawdust-H3PO4 complex [25]) was formed during impregnation. The porosity of activated carbons was greatly affected by the phosphorus-containing species incorporated in the complex and the hydrolysis of biopolymers [30].

We impregnated sawdust with H3PO4 solution in different acoustic fields and analyzed the pore properties of the as-obtained activated carbons (Table 1 ). The different pore parameters indicated that non-cavitation ultrasonic impregnation and cavitation ultrasonic impregnation had different effects on the parameters of activated carbons, as discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of non-cavitation activity on surface area and pore volume
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effects of non-cavitation on the pore structure of activated carbons, we prepared three samples (B-AC-6, B-AC-25, B-AC-44,) using the above bath-based reactor and one reference sample (AC-0) without employing ultrasound.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the obtained four ACs are presented in Fig. 7 a. The isotherms of AC-0 showed no obvious hysteresis loops and belonged to type Ib (IUPAC 2015), whereas those of B-ACs displayed hysteresis loops at relative pressure of 0.4–0.7 and belonged to a combination of type Ib and type IVa. These differences indicated that the ultrasonic vibration influenced the pore structure of activated carbons. This influence was further discussed below.

Comparison of B-AC-6 with AC-0 in FWP and BIP (Table 1) indicated that increasing the input power from 0 to 6 W increased the ultrasonic vibration energy (FWP) of the reaction system by 0.65 kPa·kHz, without changing cavitation energy (BIP). Such an increase of FWP slightly enhanced the V mic by 0.05 cm3/g (11.63%) but dramatically raised the V mes by 0.09 cm3/g (60%); consequently, the S BET and V tot increased by 13.11% and 24.14%, respectively. These results indicated that the ultrasonic vibration promoted the surface enlargement and pore development. This promotion effects could be ascribed to enhanced mass transfer caused by the pressure fluctuation during the propagation of ultrasound in mixture [31].

Further increasing the input power from 6 to 44 W increased the FWP from 0.65 to 12.01 kPa·kHz (B-AC-6 to B-AC-44). With this increase of FWP, the S BET and V mic were almost unchanged while the V mes was remarkably raised (Fig. 8 ), indicating that ultrasonic vibration mainly affected the development of V mes. More convincingly, compared with AC-0, FWP enhanced the V mes of activated carbons by 60%–100%. To gain further insight into this enhancement, we obtained pore size distribution of activated carbons, as shown in Fig. 7b. The mesopore size distribution curve of AC-0 was low and in a narrow range (2–3.2 nm), whereas that of each B-ACs was high and in a wide range (2 to higher than 4.1 nm). These facts indicated that ultrasonic vibration increased both the mesopore volume and the mesopore-size-distribution range of activated carbons. Moreover, as the FWP rose from 0.65 to 12.01 kPa·kHz, the mesopore-size-distribution curve became higher, and the upper limit for the mesopore-size-distribution range increased from 4.1 nm to 5.5 nm. Thus, enhancing the non-cavitation acoustic energy during impregnation promoted the development of mesopores, which has been found favorable for application of ACs in catalyst support [32–34] and dye absorption [35,36], etc. However, the FWP cannot be increased independently by further raising the input power, because the BIP will be increased simultaneously. Hence, it is necessary to consider the effects of cavitation activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of cavitation energy on surface area and pore volume
ce:section.ce:para: First, the FWP for B-AC-6 and H-AC-180 was the same, but the latter activated carbon was prepared with introduction of cavitation energy (BIP) during impregnation and showed more developed porosity. More specifically, the introduction of 35.07 kPa·kHz of BIP enhanced the S BET, V mic, and V mes by 186 m2/g (14.09%), 0.08 cm3/g (16.67%), and 0.08 cm3/g (33.33%), respectively. This fact indicated that ultrasonic cavitation further facilitated the surface enlargement and pore development of activated carbons.

The facilitating effect of cavitation was related to the enhancement of mass transfer caused by fragmentation of sawdust. Fragmentation of sawdust usually resulted from inter-particle collisions, shear forces, microjets, micro-steaming, and instantaneous high pressure generated by the collapse of cavitation bubbles during ultrasonic impregnation [37–39]. To check the cavitation-induced fragmentation, we compared the sawdust after horn-based ultrasonic irradiation with the one after bath-based ultrasonic irradiation and the one before irradiation in particle size distribution. The results are shown in Table 2 . Compared with the sawdust after bath-based ultrasonic irradiation or before irradiation, the sawdust after horn-based ultrasonic irradiation showed larger mass fraction in particle size ranges of below 0.3 mm and 0.3–0.42 mm, but smaller fraction in the 0.42–0.9 mm particle size range. The observation showed that the particle size of sawdust evidently decreased in horn-based ultrasonic reactor, indicating that the ultrasonic cavitation led to the reduction in particle size of sawdust. This reduction in particle size increased the surface area of sawdust and enhanced mass transfer, so the superior S BET and V mic were obtained under the cavitation condition. Another reason for the pore parameter improvement is the chemical reaction in the presence of cavitation. Sonochemical reaction occurs almost simultaneously with cavitation, during which hydroxyl radicals (radical OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and other oxidants are generated by dissociating water and oxygen [40–42]. The existence of these radicals facilitated the interaction between sawdust and H3PO4 (detailed at the beginning of Section 3.2), which contributed to the pore development of activated carbons. To check the oxidant-induced facilitation, one sample named AC-0-H2O2 was prepared in the same way that AC-0 was made, except that the used H3PO4 solution contained 1 mL of 30 wt% H2O2. The pore parameters of AC-0 and AC-0-H2O2 are shown in Table 3 . The S BET and V mic of AC-0-H2O2 increased by 9.08% and 9.30%, respectively, compared with the corresponding pore parameters of AC-0. This result confirmed that the presence of H2O2 facilitated the pore development of activated carbons.

Second, relevance of BIP and pore parameters of H-ACs (Fig. 10 , derived from Table 1) showed that the larger the BIP, the lower the S BET and V mic, which challenged the commonly-held viewpoint that ultrasonic cavitation always enhances surface area and pore volume of activated carbons. This result is attributed to the excessive cavitation bubbles, which may attach to the sawdust surface and hindered the contact of sawdust with H3PO4. Previous researchers also find this result that the excessive cavitation hinders the porosity development of activated carbons [43].

To further understand the effect mechanism of cavitation, we analyzed the pore structure of the H-ACs. H-AC-180 and H-AC-540, which were prepared under similar BIP, showed almost the same S BET and V mic but different V mes. Besides, H-AC-180 and H-AC-360 showed almost the same V mes, but the latter sample, which was prepared under much higher BIP, displayed lower S BET and V mic. The above results indicated that ultrasonic cavitation mainly affected the development of S BET and V mic, yet showed little correlation with the V mes. To observe the cavitation effect on pore size, we derived pore size distribution of H-AC-s, as shown in Fig. 9b. The micropore-size-distribution curves of H-AC-180 and H-AC-540 obtained under similar BIP were almost the same in both micropore volume and micropore distribution range. Additionally, the micropore-size-distribution curve of H-AC-180 obtained under BIP of 35.07 kPa·kHz was apparently higher than that of H-AC-360 obtained under BIP of 120.75 kPa·kHz, whereas the micropore-size-distribution range of the two samples were the same. These facts showed that ultrasonic cavitation mainly influenced the micropore volume, but hardly affected the micropore-size-distribution range of activated carbons.

To sum up, both the vibration activity and cavitation activity during ultrasonic impregnation facilitated the surface enlargement and pore development. The highest S BET (1506 m2/g), V mic (0.56 m3/g), and V mes (0.32 m3/g) for activated carbons were obtained in the presence of both FWP and BIP, which were enhanced by 29.05%, 30.23%, and 113.33%, respectively, compared with the corresponding value for AC-0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ultrasound on surface functional groups of activated carbons
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows FTIR spectra of three activated carbons (AC-0, H-AC-180, and B-AC-6) obtained at different ultrasonic impregnation conditions. As observed, the spectra of all samples showed some similar bands. The bands at 2350 and 2300 cm−1 are attributed to the CC stretch of alkyne groups [44,45]. The bands at 2100 and 1990 cm−1 are ascribed to carboxylic groups and CN vibration [45,46]. The band at 1618 cm−1 is associated with CO stretching vibration of carboxyl or carbonyl groups (e.g. ketone) [47]. The band at 1167 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching of hydrogen-bonded POOH groups from phosphates or polyphosphates, to the stretching of CO in COP (aromatic) linkage [48,49]. The band at 950 cm−1 is corresponded to the CH out-of plane bending vibration in aromatic rings [50].

Additionally, it is also noticed that there are some differences among the samples. For B-AC-6 obtained in the non-cavitation regime, compared with AC-0, the bands at 2350, 2300, and 950 cm−1 weakened, and the band at 1750 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration of carboxylic groups) disappeared. Moreover, for H-AC-180 obtained in the cavitation regime, compared with AC-0, the peaks at 2350 and 2300 cm−1 diminished, and the peaks at 950 and 1750 cm−1 disappeared. These phenomena indicated that both ultrasonic vibration activity and cavitation activity hindered the formation of alkyne groups, aromatic rings, and carboxylic groups of activated carbons. The FTIR spectra reveals that although ultrasonic impregnation hindered the formation of some functional groups, the prepared activated carbons were still rich in surface functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ultrasound on microcosmic morphology of activated carbons
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 shows the SEM images of three activated carbons (AC-0, H-AC-180, and B-AC-6) obtained under different ultrasonic impregnation conditions. All the three ACs presented similar surface morphology with tube-like micrometer-sized pores, which indicated that both ultrasonic vibration activity and cavitation activity during impregnation hardly affected microcosmic surface morphology of activated carbons. It should be mentioned that most pores of ACs are nanometer-sized inner pores; these inner pores have been characterized by N2 adsorption/desorption technique (Fig. 9), but cannot be reflected by the used SEM.

The mechanism of ultrasonic impregnation on the structural properties of activated carbon were studied and conclusions were obtained as follow: (1) The vibration activity and cavitation activity during ultrasonic impregnation both facilitated surface enlargement and pore development of activated carbons. (2) Ultrasonic vibration mainly affect the development of V mes, increasing the FWP increased both the mesopore volume and the mesopore-size-distribution range of activated carbons. (3) Ultrasonic cavitation mainly affected the development of S BET and V mic, but hardly influenced micropore-size-distribution range of activated carbons. (4) The highest S BET, V mic, and V mes for activated carbons were obtained in the presence of both the ultrasonic cavition and vibration, which were 1506 m2/g, 0.56 m3/g, and 0.32 m3/g, respectively. (5) Increasing acoustic energy during impregnation did not always favor the increment in surface area and pore volume.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51205414 and 51407200), Shandong Major R&D plan (2018GGX103034), Shandong Natural Science Foundation (ZR2017QEE006), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (14CX02199, 16CX05012A, and 18CX02121A).
